description: Test set for Tamil inscription metadata evaluation — 15 records from diverse dynasties and periods not present in train.json

inscriptions.name: Uraiyur Sangam Inscription
inscriptions.content_summary: Short Tamil-Brahmi inscription recording the dedication of a rock shelter to Jain monks by a Chola chieftain at the ancient Chola capital Uraiyur. Records the chieftain's name in archaic Tamil.
inscriptions.significance: Provides archaeological evidence for the location of the ancient Chola capital and demonstrates Jain presence at the heart of Sangam Chola territory.

inscriptions.name: Nagarjunakonda Tamil Inscription
inscriptions.content_summary: Tamil-Brahmi inscription on a Buddhist votive pillar donated by a Tamil merchant named Cantan. Records the donation of a pillar to a Buddhist stupa at Nagarjunakonda. Demonstrates Tamil participation in Buddhist patronage in the Krishna valley region.
inscriptions.significance: One of the farthest-traveled Tamil-Brahmi inscriptions; demonstrates early Tamil mercantile networks extending into the Andhra hinterland.

inscriptions.name: Mamallapuram Shore Temple Copper Plate
inscriptions.content_summary: Records tax exemptions granted to craftsmen and sculptors working on the Mamallapuram rock-cut temples. Lists names of Pallava master sculptors (sthapathis) and their guild villages. Documents the king's defeat of the Chalukya king Pulakesi II.
inscriptions.significance: Primary epigraphic record linking the Mamallapuram monuments with royal patronage; the Shore Temple is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

inscriptions.name: Perur Temple Chola Inscription
inscriptions.content_summary: Records land grants and gold ornaments donated to the Patteeswaram Shiva temple by Vikrama Chola. Documents the establishment of regular worship rituals and the appointment of 48 priests. Mentions boundary disputes with neighboring villages resolved by the Chola administration.
inscriptions.significance: Provides details of Chola-period western Tamil Nadu temple administration and land management systems.

inscriptions.name: Arittapatti Rock Inscription (Jain Beds)
inscriptions.content_summary: Tamil-Brahmi cave inscription recording the gift of a rock shelter with carved beds (palli pada) to Jain ascetics. Uses the term 'kadavan' (deity) suggesting Jain terminological influence on early Tamil. Part of a biodiversity heritage site with multiple inscriptions.
inscriptions.significance: One of the largest concentrations of Tamil-Brahmi inscriptions in a single site; now part of a declared Heritage Site protecting both the inscriptions and surrounding biodiversity.

inscriptions.name: Konerirajapuram Temple Inscription
inscriptions.content_summary: Records the construction of the Brahmapurisvara temple's principal sanctum by a Chola military commander under Sundara Chola's patronage. Describes the sculptures commissioned including 63 Nayanmars (Tamil Shaivite saints) depicted in relief. Contains the names of sculptors and their home villages.
inscriptions.significance: Contains a complete set of 63 Nayanmars depicted in stone, making it unique in early medieval Tamil Shaivite iconography.

inscriptions.name: Thanjavur Maratha Music Inscription
inscriptions.content_summary: Records Serfoji II's patronage of Carnatic music and Tamil literature. Lists the 72 melakarta ragas of Carnatic music with Tamil descriptions. Documents the establishment of the Saraswathi Mahal Library and the king's collection of 50,000+ manuscripts.
inscriptions.significance: One of the few royal inscriptions documenting musicological knowledge; the Saraswathi Mahal Library remains one of Asia's oldest manuscript repositories.

inscriptions.name: Koodal Azhagar Temple Pandya Inscription
inscriptions.content_summary: Records major donations to the Koodal Azhagar Vishnu temple including gold ornaments, golden throne, and silver vessels. Lists the Pandya king's military victories. Documents Vaishnava festivals and the Tamil commentary tradition on the Divya Prabandha sacred verses.
inscriptions.significance: Provides evidence for the growth of Vaishnava Tamil devotional culture and the performance of classical Tamil religious texts in temple settings.

inscriptions.name: Gangaikonda Cholapuram Rajaraja II Inscription
inscriptions.content_summary: Records renovation of the Brihadesvara temple at Gangaikonda Cholapuram by Rajaraja Chola II. Documents gold-plating of the vimana (tower) and gifting of jewellery to the main deity. Provides measurements of the temple's inner hall and describes architectural additions.
inscriptions.significance: Documents the continued Chola reverence for the second great Brihadesvara temple even after its political importance declined.

inscriptions.name: Thiruppuvanam Tamil Inscription
inscriptions.king: Kulottunga Chola III / Jatavarman Sundara Pandya I
inscriptions.content_summary: Documents the transfer of land rights from Chola to Pandya administrative control. Contains a rare direct reference to Chola-Pandya political transition without explicit warfare. Records donations by local chieftains switching allegiance from Chola to Pandya.
inscriptions.significance: One of the few inscriptions documenting the peaceful transfer of power from the Chola to Pandya dynasty in the early 13th century.

inscriptions.name: Vellalur Pallava Copper Plate
inscriptions.content_summary: Records land grants by the early Pallava king Simhavishnu to Brahmin scholars. Documents the settlement of northern Brahmin families in the Kongu region (western Tamil Nadu). Contains early examples of Pallava Sanskrit praśasti (praise verses) mixed with Tamil prose.
inscriptions.significance: One of the earliest Pallava copper plates; provides evidence for Brahminical migration into the Kongu-Tamil region and early Pallava administrative reach.

inscriptions.name: Kizhur Pallava Inscription
inscriptions.content_summary: Records the Pallava king Mahendravarman I's conversion from Jainism to Shaivism under the influence of Thiru Appar (Thirunavukkarasar), the Tamil Shaivite saint. One of the few epigraphic records of a royal conversion and the saint-king interaction documented in Shaiva hagiography.
inscriptions.significance: Provides rare epigraphic corroboration of an event described in Tamil Shaiva hagiographical texts (Periya Puranam); documents the Bhakti movement's influence on royalty.

inscriptions.name: Tondaimandalam Nayaka Grant
inscriptions.content_summary: Records the construction of fortifications at Gingee (Senji) and the administration of the Tondaimandalam region by Krishnappa Nayak. Documents military alliances with local Poligars and revenue assignments. Contains references to conflicts with the Bijapur Sultanate to the north.
inscriptions.significance: One of the primary records of the Gingee Nayak administration; Gingee Fort was later described as the 'Troy of the East' by the Mughals during the 17-year siege.

inscriptions.name: Padmanabhapuram Palace Tamil Inscription
inscriptions.content_summary: Bilingual Tamil-Malayalam inscription recording the construction of the Padmanabhapuram Palace complex by Travancore king Marthanda Varma. Documents the Dutch trade conflict and the Battle of Colachel (1741) in which Travancore forces defeated the Dutch East India Company — the first Asian military victory over Europeans in modern times.
inscriptions.significance: One of the only inscriptions recording the Battle of Colachel, the first defeat of a European colonial power by an Asian kingdom; the palace is a UNESCO tentative heritage site.

inscriptions.name: Sivaganga Zamindari Tamil Inscription
inscriptions.content_summary: Boundary pillar inscription from the Sivaganga estate during the reign of Queen Velu Nachiyar. Records the legal boundaries of the Sivaganga kingdom's grazing lands. Contains references to the 1780–1790 resistance against the British East India Company with mention of Haider Ali's alliance.
inscriptions.significance: One of the few surviving inscriptions from the time of Velu Nachiyar (Veeramangai), regarded as the first Indian queen to resist British colonial rule; provides geographical and political context for the Sivaganga rebellion.
